bio.education: The College of St. Scholastica '15-BS Chemistry
bio.fun_fact: Schommer is an undefeated high school wrestler
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Son of Raymond and Doris Schommer
  • All-American in cross-country skiing at St. Scholastica (Duluth, MN)
  • Has been a member of the U.S. Biathlon team since 2017
Biographical Information 

Paul Schommer grew up in Appleton, Wisconsin. He was a high school wrestler like his father until his father died in a car accident and Schommer at age 15, lost his desire to return to the mat and planned to stop competing in any sport.  

A year later, Schommer picked up cross-country skiing because his brother, Phillip, started a cross-country club at their high school. Schommer ended up as a college skier and was an All-American for The College of St. Scholastica (Duluth, Minn.).

His college coach, Chad Salmela, a former member of the U.S. biathlon national team, guided Schommer into the sport. Schommer has been a member of the U.S. biathlon national team since 2017 and competed at the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022, where he was part of a U.S. team that finished seventh in the mixed relay.

Schommer said growing up: "I don't think I ever said I wanted to be an Olympian, I grew up wanting to be a state champion in wrestling. That was a reasonable goal."

Olympic Experience
  • 2-time Olympian
    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, 5th (4 x 7.5km Relay - Men), 44th (20km Individual - Men), 47th (10km Sprint - Men), 48th (12.5km Pursuit - Men)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, 7th (4 x 6km Relay - Mixed), 13th (4 x 7.5km Relay - Men), 35th (20km Individual - Men), 74th (10km Sprint - Men)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – 9th (4 x 7.5km Relay - Men), 24th (20km Individual - Men), 39th (12.5km Pursuit - Men), 56th (10km Sprint - Men)
  • Years of participation: 10km Sprint - Men 2020, 2021, 2023, 2025; 12.5km Pursuit - Men 2023, 2025; 20km Individual - Men 2020, 2021, 2023, 2025; 4 x 7.5km Relay - Men 2020, 2021, 2023, 2025; 4 x 6km Relay - Mixed 2023; Single Mixed Relay - Mixed 2023
  • Top finish: 8th – 2020 (4 x 7.5km Relay - Men)

Did You Know?: Nathan will graduate high school in 2010 and has already earned university sophomore status...his coach is world champion Jan Olesinski who has coached athletes to world and Olympic levels...Nathan's brother, Lucas, and Jan's daughter, Anna, are all part of "Team Roswell" pentathlon...Nathan is a wildlife enthusiast...he is an expert on bird identification and it is not unusual to find him handling snakes and lizards as if there was nothing to it...he helped raise a pronghorn antelope and enjoyed racing across the desert with the fastest land animal in North America (the antelope usually won)...Nathan lives on a cattle ranch near Roswell, N.M....his family raises Brangus cattle, Paint and Andalusian horses, and they also farm and research the desert Mule deer...Nathan is no stranger to the hard, physical work that ranch life requires and this hard work has paid off toward his pentathlon accomplishments...he has a strong faith in God and believes that he should do pentathlon, or whatever else he is doing, with all his heart, "As unto the Lord."

bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Schuble has been a talented athlete throughout her life. She was a two-time state champion in soccer and a varsity track runner in high school. While attending the United States Military Academy at West Point to become a commissions officer, Schuble was a varsity athlete in three separate sports. One day during hand-to-hand combat class, she sustained a traumatic brain injury. She sustained an additional TBI later in a car wreck, and in 2004, she was diagnosed with multiple sclerosis. This led her to the Lakeshore Foundation, a Paralympic training site in Birmingham where she was encouraged to get into cycling, which she did in 2007. In 2008, she won a gold medal and set a world record in the 500m time trial at the Paralympic Games Beijing 2008. She was the first female in the world to compete in team sprint as Team USA was only country to do it. At the London 2012 Paralympic Games, she was the first woman to medal as a part of team sprint. She continued her success at the Paralympic Games Rio 2016.
  • Daughter of Jerry and Beth Schuble
  • Has one brother, Tom, and two sisters, Stephanie and Melissa
  • Has two English bulldogs, Panzer and Sherman
  • Grandfather and his brother both played professional baseball
  • Works as an engineer
  • Hobbies include cycling, triathlons and playing with her bulldogs
Paralympic Experience
  • 3-time Paralympian; 5-time Paralympic medalist (1 gold, 3 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, 4th (C1-5 Team Sprint - Mixed), 5th (C5 - Time Trial - Women), 6th (C5 - 3km Pursuit - Women), 7th (C4-5 - 500m Time Trial - Women), 8th (C4-5 - Road Race - Women)
    • Paralympic Games London 2012, silver (C4-5 - 500m Time Trial - Women), bronze (C1-5 Team Sprint - Mixed), 5th (C4-5 - Road Race - Women, C5 - 3km Pursuit - Women), 6th (C5 - Time Trial - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Beijing 2008, gold (LC1/LC2/CP4 - 500m Time Trial - Women), silver (LC1/LC2/CP4 - 3km Pursuit - Women, LC1/LC2/CP4 - Time Trial - Women)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – 8th (C4-5 - Scratch Race - Women, C5 - 1000m Time Trial - Women, C5 - Elimination - Women)
  • Years of participation: C4-5 - Scratch Race - Women 2017, 2018, 2025; C5 - 1000m Time Trial - Women 2025; C5 - Elimination - Women 2025; C1-5 Team Sprint - Open 2009, 2012, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018; C5 - 3km Pursuit - Women 2011, 2012, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018; C5 - 500m Time Trial - Women 2011, 2012, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018; C5 - Road Race - Women 2010, 2014, 2017, 2018; C5 - Time Trial - Women 2014, 2017, 2018; C5 - Scratch Race - Women 2016; C1-5 - Scratch Race - Women 2014; LC2/CP4 - 3km Pursuit - Women 2007, 2009; LC2/CP4 - 500m Time Trial - Women 2007, 2009; LC2/CP4 - Road Race - Women 2009; LC2/CP4 - Time Trial - Women 2007, 2009; LC2 - Road Race - Women 2007
  • Medals: 15 (6 gold, 4 silver, 5 bronze)
    • Gold – 2017 (C5 - 500m Time Trial - Women); 2009 (LC2/CP4 - Road Race - Women, LC2/CP4 - 3km Pursuit - Women, LC2/CP4 - 500m Time Trial - Women); 2007 (LC2 - Road Race - Women, LC2/CP4 - Time Trial - Women)
    • Silver – 2017 (C5 - 3km Pursuit - Women); 2014 (C5 - 500m Time Trial - Women); 2009 (LC2/CP4 - Time Trial - Women); 2007 (LC2/CP4 - 3km Pursuit - Women)

bio.fun_fact: First person to win a gold medal at the Summer and Winter X Games
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Speciality: Banked Slalom and Snowboard Cross
  • Son of Scott Schultz and Carrie Johnson
  • Married to Sara Schultz
  • Has one daughter, Lauren
  • The entire U.S. Paralympic snowboard team in the LL-1 and LL-2 classes uses his company's prosthetic knee (Moto Knee) and foot (Versa Foot)
  • Hobbies include motocross, mountain biking and horseback riding
Biographical Information

Mike Schultz grew up on a farm in Minnesota racing everything from BMX bikes to snowmobiles. He also developed a second passion in life behind racing which was designing and creating things, something his father helped nurtured by teaching him to weld at age 10. In 2008, while Schultz was competing in a professional snow-cross race, he drifted off course, flying off his snowmobile and landing on his left leg. He sustained a severe compound fracture to his knee, after multiple surgeries, had his leg amputated above the knee. Mixing his background in action sports with his passion for creating things, he developed and patented Moto Knee and other prosthetics, which was able to absorb the amount of impact needed to be an action sport athlete, under his new company, BioDapt.

In 2010, Schultz became the first person to win a gold medal at the Summer and Winter X Games in adaptive motocross and snowmobile cross, respectively. Taking up snowboarding after the accident, Schultz joined the U.S. Para Snowboard Team in 2015 and made his Paralympic debut at the Paralympic Winter Games PyeongChang 2018, winning gold in snowboard cross and silver in banked slalom. Today about 95% of the top Paralympic lower-limb snowboard athletes use BioDapt's products including the entire U.S. Paralympic snowboard team.

When he is not on the snow, Schultz spends time on his Minnesota farm riding a dirtbike or one of six horses and spending with his wife, Sara, and daughter, Lauren.

Paralympic Experience
  • 3-time Paralympian; 4-time Paralympic medalist (1 gold, 2 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Milan 2026, bronze (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men), 6th (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Beijing 2022, silver (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men), 5th (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Pyeongchang 2018, gold (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men), silver (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men)
World Championships Experience

  • Years of participation: Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men 2017, 2019, 2022, 2025; Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men 2017, 2019, 2022, 2023, 2025; Dual Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men 2023; Dual Banked Slalom - Team - Men 2023; Snowboard Cross Team Event - Men 2019, 2022
  • Medals: 7 (1 gold, 4 silver, 2 bronze)
    • Gold – 2019 (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men)
    • Silver – 2023 (Dual Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men); 2022 (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men, Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men); 2017 (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men)
    • Bronze – 2023 (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men); 2019 (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men)

Quick Facts
  • Graduated from the University of Illinois in December of 2005 with a bachelor’s degree in community health
  • Shortly after graduating, she moved to Denver, Colorado, to start the Denver Rolling Nuggets, a women's wheelchair basketball team
  • Was the head coach of the U.S. National Women's Wheelchair Basketball Team for the Paralympic Games Paris 2024, helping earn a silver medal
  • Hobbies include reading and coaching
Biographical Information

The U.S. women’s wheelchair basketball team won its first gold medal in 22 years at the Paralympic Games Athens 2004. Christina (Ripp) Schwab and Stephanie Wheeler went on to coach future national teams, Wheeler in 2016 and 2020, and Schwab in 2024. Both are members of the National Wheelchair Basketball Association Hall of Fame. Six members of the 2004 team (Emily Hoskins, Becca Cisneros, Kerri Morgan Hoffman, Wheeler, Schwab, and Desi Warkins) returned to win gold again at the Beijing 2008 Paralympic Games, marking the first back-to-back titles for the program. In 2004, the U.S. defeated five-time defending champion Canada in a tough semifinal and avenged a narrow group-stage loss to Australia with a 56–44 victory in the gold-medal game, setting the stage for continued success in future Paralympic Games.

bio.education: Forest Hill Collegiate Institute (Toronto, Canada) ‘03 University of Waterloo ‘08, Computer Science
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Born in Toronto, Schwartz started skiing at a young age and began her racing career in 2007. As a congenital leg amputee who is also missing most of her right hip, she races on one ski with two outriggers, which are poles with ski tips at the bottom. At the Paralympic Winter Games Vancouver 2010, she skied in four events for Team Canada. In 2012, she transitioned to the U.S. Paralympics Alpine Skiing National Team. She landed a spot on her first U.S. Paralympic Team in 2014 for the Paralympic Winter Games Sochi
  • She enjoys giving back to her community through volunteer service
  • She volunteered with the War Amps CHAMP (Child Amputee) program for eight years
  • Has worked with a variety of programming languages on several platforms in both classroom and professional environments
  • She worked full time as a product database specialist
  • Daughter of Dale and Jay Schwartz
  • Has three siblings, Rachel, Robyn and Aleksandra
  • Hobbies include cycling, cooking and going on various types of adventures
  • Speaks fluent French
  • Credits Swiss alpine skier Didier Cuche as her idol.
Paralympic Experience
  • 3-time Paralympian
    • Paralympic Games Pyeongchang 2018, 7th (Downhill - Standing - Women), 8th (Super Combined - Standing - Women, Super-G - Standing - Women), 11th (Slalom - Standing - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Sochi 2014, (Super Combined - Standing - Women), 9th (Super-G - Standing - Women), 10th (Slalom - Standing - Women), 14th (Giant Slalom - Standing - Women)
    • Paralympic Games Vancouver 2010, 10th (Super Combined - Standing - Women), 12th (Giant Slalom - Standing - Women), 13th (Slalom - Standing - Women), 14th (Super-G - Standing - Women)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2017 – 7th (Downhill - Standing - Women), 8th (Super Combined - Standing - Women), 9th (Giant Slalom - Standing - Women, Slalom - Standing - Women), 10th (Super-G - Standing - Women)
  • Years of participation: Downhill - Standing - Women 2015, 2017; Giant Slalom - Standing - Women 2015, 2017; Slalom - Standing - Women 2015, 2017; Super Combined - Standing - Women 2015, 2017; Super-G - Standing - Women 2015, 2017
  • Top finish: – 2015 (Downhill - Standing - Women), 2015 (Giant Slalom - Standing - Women)
